seasons.accTournament: Champions (beat Boston College 78-76 in final; K’s 1,000th career game; 10th ACC Tournament title)
seasons.ncaaTournament: Sweet Sixteen (#1 seed Atlanta Regional; beat Southern 80-55, George Washington 74-61; lost to LSU 62-54)
seasons.tagline: The Grand Finale That Ended Too Soon. Redick breaks every scoring record in ACC history. Shelden Williams wins DPOY again. The most-watched college basketball game in ESPN history takes place on their Senior Night — and they lose it. K coaches his 1,000th game. Then Garrett Temple shuts down the greatest shooter Duke has ever produced, and the dream dies in the Sweet Sixteen.
seasons.overview:
The 2005-06 season was supposed to be the coronation. J.J. Redick and Shelden Williams returned as seniors, both All-Americans, both among the most decorated players in program history. The freshmen class added real talent: Josh McRoberts, a 6-10 forward from Indianapolis who could handle the ball like a guard, and Greg Paulus, a point guard who had turned down a scholarship to play quarterback at Notre Dame. Duke was the preseason #1 team in the country. The expectations were a national championship — nothing less.

Redick delivered the greatest individual season in Duke history. He averaged 26.8 points per game, second in the nation behind only Gonzaga’s Adam Morrison, and set records that may never be broken. On February 14, against Wake Forest, he surpassed Virginia’s Curtis Staples to set the NCAA career record for three-pointers made (he would finish with 457). Five days later, he scored 30 against Miami to become Duke’s all-time leading scorer with 2,557 points, passing Dick Groat. On February 25, against Temple, he passed Dickie Hemric’s 51-year-old ACC scoring record of 2,587 points. He had three 40-point games in a single season — unprecedented in the modern ACC. He finished his career with 2,769 points (the most in ACC history at the time, later passed by Tyler Hansbrough), a .912 career free throw percentage (the best in NCAA history among players with 600+ makes), and 964 points in his senior season alone, just six short of Dennis Scott’s ACC single-season record of 970.

Shelden Williams won the NABC National Defensive Player of the Year award for the second consecutive season, becoming only the second player in history to win it twice. He was a first-team AP All-American — the ninth set of Duke teammates to be named AP first-team All-Americans simultaneously, the first since Jason Williams and Shane Battier in 2001. Williams finished his career as Duke’s all-time leader in rebounds and blocked shots, with 59 career double-doubles and one of only three triple-doubles in school history.

The regular season was dominant: 14-2 in the ACC, the regular season championship, and the #1 ranking for most of the year. Duke was never ranked lower than third nationally. The Blue Devils won the NIT Season Tip-Off and rolled through the conference schedule with the kind of authority that suggested a deep March run. But there were cracks.

The first came at Chapel Hill on February 8, when Duke beat UNC 87-83 behind 35 points from Redick. He was unconscious — hitting shots from everywhere, torching the Carolina backcourt of Bobby Frasor and Wes Miller. It was the kind of performance that made everyone watching think the championship was inevitable.

The second crack came on Senior Night at Cameron Indoor Stadium on March 4 — and this time it was a fracture. Duke was 27-2, ranked #1, honoring Redick, Williams, Sean Dockery, and Lee Melchionni in what was expected to be a coronation. North Carolina was the 13th-ranked team, a freshmen-heavy squad that had lost its seven leading scorers from the 2005 national championship team. Sports Illustrated hadn’t even put them in the projected NCAA Tournament field in the preseason.

It became the most-watched college basketball game in ESPN history.

Redick hit four of his first five shots and Duke jumped to a 13-2 lead. Then freshman Marcus Ginyard checked in and spent the rest of the game harassing Redick, who missed 15 of his final 16 shots and finished 5-of-21. Meanwhile, freshman Tyler Hansbrough — playing against Shelden Williams, the two-time National Defensive Player of the Year — scored 27 points and grabbed 10 rebounds. Carolina broke a 59-59 tie with an 11-0 run over three and a half minutes. Danny Green came up with a key defensive stop on Sean Dockery’s floater in the final minute. Bobby Frasor and Hansbrough nailed six straight free throws to ice it: UNC 83, Duke 76. The most telling stat: Carolina’s four freshmen scored 55 points. Duke’s four senior starters scored 51.

Roy Williams told his team before the game: “I hope it isn’t cockiness, but I told them that I thought we were the only team in the country that could come in here and win this game.” Hansbrough said afterward: “When I knocked down those free throws, it hit me that we’d just come in here and beat Duke. It doesn’t get any better than this.” It was UNC’s first win at Cameron since 2001. It was the first of four wins Hansbrough’s teams would record in Durham — he never lost at Cameron Indoor Stadium in his career.

Duke regrouped for the ACC Tournament in Greensboro. The championship game against Boston College was K’s 1,000th career game as a head coach, and he celebrated it with a 78-76 victory. Redick hit a three to tie with 1:53 left, then another with 1:17 remaining to take the lead for good. He won the ACC Tournament MVP for the second consecutive year — only the fourth player in league history to do so — and reclaimed the NCAA three-point record that St. Peter’s Keydren Clark had briefly stolen during the MAAC Tournament. It was K’s 10th ACC Tournament championship.

In the NCAA Tournament, Duke was the #1 seed in the Atlanta Regional. The Blue Devils dispatched Southern 80-55 and George Washington 74-61 to set up a Sweet Sixteen matchup with fourth-seeded LSU.

Then Garrett Temple happened.

LSU coach John Brady designed a game plan around one principle: Temple, a 6-5 guard with long arms and elite athleticism, would chase Redick everywhere. Through every screen. Around every curl. Over every pick. The strategy was the same one Michigan State had used the year before, but Temple executed it with a relentlessness that bordered on artistry. Redick — the #2 scorer in the nation, the consensus National Player of the Year, the greatest shooter in ACC history — shot 3-of-18 from the field and scored 11 points. It was one of the worst performances of his career, and it came in the last game he would ever play at Duke.

LSU won 62-54. Duke’s season ended at 32-4. The record over Redick’s four years was 116-23. He won every major National Player of the Year award: the Wooden, the Naismith, the AP, the USBWA, the NABC, the Rupp, Basketball Times, The Sporting News. He also won the James E. Sullivan Award as the top amateur athlete in the United States — the first Duke player to receive that honor. Williams was the 5th overall pick in the 2006 NBA Draft. Redick was selected 11th by the Orlando Magic.

But the championship never came. In four years at Duke, Redick made it to the Final Four once (2004, lost to UConn), the Sweet Sixteen twice (2005, lost to Michigan State; 2006, lost to LSU), and missed the Sweet Sixteen once (2003, lost to Kansas). For all the individual brilliance — 2,769 points, 457 three-pointers, a .912 free throw percentage, two National Player of the Year awards, four All-America selections — the one thing that eluded him was a ring.

The irony would take eighteen years to ripen. In June 2024, Redick was named head coach of the Los Angeles Lakers — the same franchise that had tried to take Coach K away from Duke the summer before Redick’s sophomore year. Krzyzewski, who stayed at Duke because his heart belonged there, served as an unofficial advisor during the search. Redick, whose career at Duke was saved by K’s decision to stay, was now leading the team K turned down.

Some circles close. This one just took a while.

unc.story: Redick put on a clinic. He scored 35 points, torching UNC’s backcourt of Bobby Frasor and Wes Miller all night. He hit shots from everywhere — off screens, in transition, pulling up in traffic. Carolina, with its freshmen-heavy roster, hung around behind David Noel and Reyshawn Terry, but Redick was simply too much. Duke won 87-83, and it felt like a statement: this team, with this version of Redick, was going to be impossible to stop in March. Everyone believed it. Including Redick.

The most-watched college basketball game in ESPN history. Senior Night for Redick, Williams, Dockery, and Melchionni. Duke 27-2, ranked #1, heavy favorites. UNC’s freshmen-dominated team had been picked sixth in the ACC preseason and left off Sports Illustrated’s projected NCAA Tournament field.

Redick hit four of his first five shots. Duke jumped to 13-2. It looked like a coronation.

Then Marcus Ginyard checked in and spent the rest of the game harassing Redick into missing 15 of his final 16 shots. Tyler Hansbrough, a freshman playing against the two-time National Defensive Player of the Year, scored 27 points and grabbed 10 rebounds. Carolina broke a 59-59 tie with an 11-0 run. Danny Green blocked Dockery’s floater in the final minute. Bobby Frasor and Hansbrough made six straight free throws. UNC 83, Duke 76.

The stat that told the story: Carolina’s four freshmen scored 55. Duke’s four senior starters scored 51.

Roy Williams before the game: “I told them I thought we were the only team in the country that could come in here and win this game.” Hansbrough after: “When I knocked down those free throws, it hit me that we’d just come in here and beat Duke. It doesn’t get any better than this.”

It was UNC’s first win at Cameron since 2001. It was the first of four wins Hansbrough’s teams would record in Durham. He never lost at Cameron Indoor Stadium in his entire career. And it was the beginning of the most decorated class in UNC history: 124-22, three ACC regular season titles, two ACC Tournament titles, two Final Fours, and the 2009 national championship. It all started on Redick’s Senior Night.

theGame.title: Garrett Temple Shuts Down the Dream: LSU in the Sweet Sixteen
theGame.headline: LSU’s Garrett Temple chases Redick through every screen for 40 minutes, holding the #2 scorer in the nation to 3-of-18 shooting and 11 points. The greatest individual career in Duke history ends not with a ring, but with a quiet walk to the locker room in Atlanta.
theGame.story:
The scouting report had been written the year before by Michigan State, and refined on Senior Night by Marcus Ginyard. If you could get a long, athletic defender to chase J.J. Redick through every screen — fight over every pick, deny every catch, contest every curl — you could take him out of a game. The problem was that almost nobody had the personnel or the discipline to do it for 40 minutes. In the 2006 Sweet Sixteen, LSU did.

John Brady’s Tigers were a fourth seed with nothing to lose and a defensive identity built around versatile athletes. His weapon was Garrett Temple, a 6-5 sophomore guard with long arms, quick feet, and the kind of relentless motor that turned chasing Redick from a strategy into a personal mission. Temple shadowed Redick everywhere. Through screens. Around picks. Into the corners. Off the ball. The coverage was suffocating.

Redick — the consensus National Player of the Year, the #2 scorer in the nation at 26.8 points per game, the all-time leading scorer in Duke and ACC history — shot 3-of-18 from the field. He scored 11 points. It was one of the worst performances of his career, and it was his last game in a Duke uniform.

The rest of the team couldn’t compensate. Shelden Williams fought for 23 points and 8 rebounds, but the offense was stagnant without Redick’s gravity pulling defenders away from the paint. Josh McRoberts and Greg Paulus, the freshmen who were supposed to provide the depth that last year’s team lacked, couldn’t generate enough offense against LSU’s physical, switching defense. Duke scored 54 points — its lowest total of the season.

LSU won 62-54. It was over.

The post-game scene was quiet. Redick, who had spent four years as the most visible, most polarizing, most scrutinized player in college basketball — a man who received 50 to 75 hate calls per day after opponents discovered his cell phone number, who was called “the most hated current athlete in America” by a CBS Sports columnist, who wrote slam poetry in his spare time and had Bible verses tattooed on his arms — walked off the court for the last time.

His career totals: 2,769 points (ACC record at the time), 457 three-pointers (NCAA record at the time), .912 career free throw percentage (NCAA record for 600+ makes), 964 points in his senior season alone, two National Player of the Year awards, four All-America selections, the James E. Sullivan Award as the top amateur athlete in the United States. A 116-23 record over four years. And no championship.

Shelden Williams: Duke’s all-time leader in rebounds and blocked shots. Two-time National Defensive Player of the Year. First-team AP All-American. 59 career double-doubles. The 5th overall pick in the 2006 NBA Draft.

Together, they were the greatest duo of the Coach K era that never won a title. Their numbers hang in the Cameron rafters side by side — #4 and #23 — retired in back-to-back ceremonies in January and February of 2007.

Redick went on to play 15 NBA seasons, became a pioneering podcast host, and in June 2024 was named head coach of the Los Angeles Lakers. Williams was drafted 5th overall by the Atlanta Hawks and had a six-year NBA career before moving into basketball operations.

The 2005-06 season was the end of an era in every sense. After Redick and Williams graduated, Duke would field its youngest team in more than 60 years. The Transition was over. What came next would look nothing like what had come before.

accTourney.story: Redick scored 25 points against Miami in the ACC Tournament quarterfinal, setting the Duke single-season scoring record at 858 points. He would finish the season with 964. The victory also saw him reclaim the NCAA career three-point record that St. Peter’s Keydren Clark had briefly taken during the MAAC Tournament, hitting his 15th three of the ACC Tournament.
Duke advanced past NC State in the semifinal to set up a championship game against Boston College — the 1,000th game of K’s career.

K’s 1,000th career game as a head coach. Boston College stormed back to take the lead, but Redick hit a three to tie with 1:53 remaining and another with 1:17 left to take the lead for good. Duke won 78-76. It was K’s 10th ACC Tournament championship. Redick won Tournament MVP for the second consecutive year, only the fourth player in league history to do so. He finished his career as the all-time leading scorer in ACC Tournament history with 225 points, passing Wake Forest’s Len Chappell.

ncaaTourney.story: Duke handled Southern comfortably in the first round. A business-like 25-point victory that felt like a formality for a team with championship ambitions.

Duke advanced past George Washington 74-61, earning a Sweet Sixteen berth for the fourth time in Redick and Williams’ four-year careers. The Blue Devils looked sharp and confident heading into the regional semifinal.

Garrett Temple. LSU’s 6-5 sophomore guard spent 40 minutes chasing Redick through every screen, around every pick, into every corner. The consensus National Player of the Year shot 3-of-18 and scored 11 points — Duke’s lowest output of the season. Shelden Williams fought for 23 and 8, but nobody else could generate offense against LSU’s physical, switching defense. The greatest individual career in Duke history ended in the Georgia Dome in Atlanta. The championship that Redick, Williams, and K had spent four years pursuing never came. Redick’s career: 2,769 points, 457 threes, .912 FT%, two NPOYs, four All-America nods, 116-23 record. And no ring.

seasons.ncaaTournament: First Round (#6 seed; lost to #11 VCU 79-77)
seasons.tagline: Rock Bottom. Only 27% of scoring returns. The youngest team in 60 years. Two sophomores named captain for the first time in program history. Gerald Henderson bloodies Tyler Hansbrough’s nose and gets ejected. And Eric Maynor hits a jumper with 1.8 seconds left to give VCU the most devastating first-round upset in Duke’s modern era.
seasons.overview:
The numbers were bleak. Only 39.0% of minutes played and a staggering 27.2% of scoring returned from the 2005-06 roster. Redick (2,769 career points), Shelden Williams (all-time rebounds and blocks leader), Sean Dockery, and Lee Melchionni were all gone. Duke fielded ten freshmen or sophomores out of twelve roster players — the youngest team in more than 60 years. There was not a single senior on the roster.

The only upperclassman was junior DeMarcus Nelson, who had been injury-plagued in his first two seasons and entered the year with just nine career starts. The most experienced players were sophomores Greg Paulus (33 career starts) and Josh McRoberts (31 starts). For the first time in the 102-year history of Duke men’s basketball, two sophomores — Paulus and McRoberts — were named team captains. The freshman class included Gerald Henderson, a 6-5 shooting guard from Philadelphia; Jon Scheyer, a cerebral combo guard from the Chicago suburbs; Lance Thomas, a versatile forward; and Brian Zoubek, a 7-1 center from New Jersey.

Then the preseason dealt another blow. Paulus, the team’s most experienced player and expected floor general, broke his foot in October and missed all of preseason practice. He returned in time for the opener but spent months trying to mesh with teammates he had barely practiced with. The team never fully gelled.

Nelson stepped up admirably, averaging a team-high 14.5 points and shooting over 50% from the field, but he couldn’t carry the offense alone. McRoberts showed flashes of the versatile, do-everything talent that would make him a first-round NBA pick, but at 6-10 he was the team’s only real interior presence — Zoubek was too raw as a freshman to carry significant minutes. The Blue Devils averaged just 68.9 points per game, their lowest offensive output in the K era, though the defense remained characteristically stingy at 53.4 points allowed early in the season.

Duke started 13-1 and looked like it might defy the youth narrative. But ACC play exposed the roster’s limitations. The Blue Devils went 8-8 in conference — their first .500 ACC record since the 1994-95 nightmare season when K had missed time with back surgery. They finished tied for fourth. The margin for error that Redick and Williams had provided for years was gone, and when opponents pressed Paulus or sagged into the paint against McRoberts, there was nobody else to turn to.

The ACC Tournament brought an immediate exit — a first-round loss that offered no salvation. Duke limped into the NCAA Tournament as a #6 seed, their lowest seeding since 1998.

The first-round opponent was 11th-seeded VCU, coached by Jeff Capel — a Duke alumnus, former player under K, and the man whose half-court shot had sent the 1995 Duke-UNC game into double overtime. Capel’s Rams were 27-6, running an aggressive, up-tempo style with a fearless point guard named Eric Maynor.

The game in Buffalo was a war. Three players left with blood — Henderson cut his hand, Scheyer was cut near his eye by Maynor’s elbow, and VCU’s Wil Fameni got a bloody nose from a teammate collision. Paulus played 38 minutes under relentless full-court pressure and scored a career-high 25 points. McRoberts recorded a double-double with 22 points and 12 rebounds, adding 5 blocks and 4 assists in 40 minutes. They became the first pair of Duke teammates to both score 20 points all season.

Duke led by 11 in the second half at 61-50 after holding VCU scoreless for five and a half minutes. Then the Rams erupted with an 18-6 run to take a 68-67 lead with five minutes remaining. The game was tied at 70, 72, 74, and 77 in the final minutes. VCU led by two with 16 seconds left when Nelson took a missed free throw the length of the court and scored to tie it at 77 with 12 seconds remaining.

VCU called timeout. Maynor took the inbound, dribbled to the right side of the lane, and hit a pull-up jumper with 1.8 seconds left. The ball went through clean. Paulus’ desperation 40-footer at the buzzer fell just left of the basket. VCU 79, Duke 77.

It was Duke’s first NCAA Tournament first-round loss since 1996. It broke a streak of nine consecutive Sweet Sixteen appearances — the longest active run in the NCAA by seven years and the second-longest in tournament history. The 22-11 record was Duke’s worst since K’s health-related absence in 1994-95.

McRoberts declared for the NBA Draft after the season, selected 37th overall by the Portland Trail Blazers. His departure removed the team’s most versatile player but opened the door for the freshmen who would form the nucleus of Duke’s next chapter: Henderson, Scheyer, Thomas, and eventually Kyle Singler and Nolan Smith in 2007-08.

The 2006-07 season was the price Duke paid for the Redick-Williams era’s brilliance. Two players had carried so much of the program’s identity that their departure left a vacuum no freshman class could immediately fill. It was also a reminder that even at Duke, under the greatest coach in the history of the sport, talent and experience matter. You can coach up potential, but you can’t manufacture it overnight.

K later used the 2006-07 season as a teaching tool. The VCU loss, he told future teams, was what happened when you didn’t have an identity, when you relied on individual performances instead of collective habits, when you were talented but not yet tough. The freshman core that survived that season — Henderson, Scheyer, Thomas, Zoubek — would internalize that lesson. Three years later, they would win a national championship.

unc.story: The first UNC game without Redick and Williams in four years, and the gap was immediately evident. North Carolina, now led by sophomore Tyler Hansbrough and Roy Williams’ deepening roster, controlled the game. Hansbrough continued his dominance at Cameron — a building where he would go 4-0 in his career. Duke’s young backcourt couldn’t generate enough offense to keep pace. UNC won and served notice that the balance of power on Tobacco Road had shifted decisively.

The Bloody Nose Game. With 14.5 seconds remaining and the outcome already decided, freshman Gerald Henderson went for a hard foul on Tyler Hansbrough as the UNC center grabbed a rebound. Henderson’s elbow connected with Hansbrough’s face, breaking his nose and sending blood streaming down his jersey. Hansbrough jumped up immediately, enraged, before teammate Dewey Burke calmed him. Henderson was charged with a combative foul and ejected.

Roy Williams called it “an unbelievably vicious game” and said both teams had played at a ferocious intensity level throughout. Henderson and Hansbrough have both said since that the foul was unintentional. Henderson, who had watched Hansbrough dominate Duke as a high school senior the year before, later said: “I remember watching the game and being like, ‘This dude is an animal, man.’”

Hansbrough finished with 26 points and 17 rebounds — and one broken nose. He wore a protective face mask through the ACC Tournament and into the second round of the NCAA Tournament. The image of his blood-covered face became one of the most iconic photographs in rivalry history. UNC completed the season sweep, 86-72. The two teams wouldn’t meet again until the following February, when Henderson and Hansbrough would share a court again with years of unfinished business between them.

Years later, the two launched a podcast together about the Duke-UNC rivalry. Some things are bigger than the blood.

theGame.title: Maynor’s Dagger: VCU in the First Round
theGame.headline: Duke leads by 11 in the second half. VCU storms back. Nelson’s miracle layup ties it at 77 with 12 seconds left. Then Eric Maynor hits a pull-up jumper with 1.8 seconds remaining. Paulus’ 40-footer misses left. The longest Sweet Sixteen streak in NCAA history is over.
theGame.story:
The cruelest part of the 2006-07 season wasn’t the loss itself — it was how close Duke came to surviving it.

The Blue Devils, a #6 seed, faced 11th-seeded VCU in Buffalo in a first-round game that had all the makings of an upset. The Rams were 27-6, coached by Jeff Capel — a Duke alumnus who had played under K and whose half-court buzzer-beater had sent the 1995 Duke-UNC game into double overtime. Capel knew Duke’s strengths and weaknesses as intimately as anyone in the country. His team ran an aggressive, pressing style with a fearless sophomore point guard named Eric Maynor.

The game was physical from the opening tip. Three players left the court bleeding at various points — Henderson’s hand, Scheyer’s eye (from Maynor’s elbow), and VCU’s Fameni’s nose. Paulus and Maynor got tangled multiple times in a running battle that lasted 40 minutes. “It was a physical game throughout,” Paulus said. “They’re a physical team and we’re not going to back down to anybody.”

Paulus scored a career-high 25. McRoberts had 22 points, 12 rebounds, 5 blocks, and 4 assists in 40 minutes. It was the finest game of his Duke career — and no other Blue Devil reached double figures. The Big Two did everything they could. It wasn’t enough.

Duke built an 11-point lead at 61-50 in the second half after holding VCU scoreless for five and a half minutes. The game felt won. Then VCU unleashed an 18-6 run. Maynor found seams. Shooters hit open threes. The press that had bothered Duke all game turned turnovers into fast-break points. With five minutes remaining, VCU led 68-67. The lead changed hands six more times.

With 16 seconds left, VCU led 79-75 after two free throws. DeMarcus Nelson grabbed the miss on a VCU free throw, raced the length of the court, and scored to make it 79-77 with 12 seconds remaining. VCU called timeout.

The inbound went to Maynor. He dribbled right, sized up the defense, and pulled up from just inside the free throw line. The jumper was clean. It fell through with 1.8 seconds left. VCU 79, Duke 77.

Duke called timeout. Paulus caught the inbound and heaved a 40-footer that fell just left of the basket at the buzzer.

The streak was over. Nine consecutive Sweet Sixteen appearances — the longest in the NCAA by seven years, the second-longest in tournament history — broken by an 11th seed in the first round. It was Duke’s first opening-round loss since 1996, the last time the program had been this young and this thin.

McRoberts left for the NBA Draft. But the freshmen who survived this game — Gerald Henderson, Jon Scheyer, Lance Thomas, Brian Zoubek — carried the scar of Buffalo with them. Three years later, in 2010, those four players would be seniors on a Duke team that won the national championship. K made sure they never forgot what the VCU loss felt like. He used it as a teaching tool for years: what happens when you have talent but no identity, when you’re young and physical but not yet tough, when you lead by 11 and think the game is over.

The 2006-07 season was the bottom. Everything that came after — the reload, the recruiting, the 2010 championship — was built on its ashes.

accTourney.story: Duke’s ACC Tournament ended immediately with a first-round exit. For a team that had won the tournament the previous year in K’s 1,000th game, it was a jarring fall. The Blue Devils limped into the NCAA Tournament bracket as a #6 seed — their lowest since 1998.

ncaaTourney.story: The end of an era. Duke led by 11 in the second half. VCU stormed back with an 18-6 run. Paulus scored a career-high 25, McRoberts had 22/12/5 blocks/4 assists. Nobody else reached double figures. Nelson’s miracle layup tied it at 77 with 12 seconds left. Eric Maynor — playing for Jeff Capel, a Duke alum who once hit a half-court shot to force OT against UNC — drained a pull-up jumper with 1.8 seconds remaining. Paulus’ 40-footer missed left. VCU 79, Duke 77. First NCAA first-round loss since 1996. The nine-year Sweet Sixteen streak — the longest in the NCAA by seven years — was over. Duke finished 22-11, its worst record since K’s health absence in 1994-95. The freshmen who survived this game won a national championship three years later.

seasons.ncaaTournament: Second Round (#2 seed West; beat Belmont 71-70; lost to West Virginia 73-67)
seasons.tagline: The False Dawn. Duke starts 21-1, climbs to #2 in the country, and looks reborn. Kyle Singler and Nolan Smith arrive. Henderson becomes a star. Then the team goes 7-5 down the stretch, barely survives Belmont by a point, and gets physically manhandled out of the tournament by West Virginia. The rebuild isn’t over. It’s just getting started.
seasons.overview:
The VCU loss had left a scar, but the 2007-08 roster had the ingredients for a genuine bounce-back. With 82.0% of minutes and 81.3% of scoring returning, plus a highly-rated freshman class headlined by Kyle Singler and Nolan Smith, Duke had both experience and new talent for the first time since the Redick-Williams era.

Senior DeMarcus Nelson was the sole captain and the team’s emotional leader. He had waited three years behind Redick and Williams and endured injury-plagued early seasons to finally command the stage. Junior Greg Paulus ran the point. Sophomore Gerald Henderson, the player whose elbow had broken Tyler Hansbrough’s nose eight months earlier, was emerging as Duke’s most dynamic athlete — a 6-5 wing with NBA-caliber explosiveness who averaged 12.7 points and 1.1 blocks per game. Sophomore Jon Scheyer operated as the sixth man, providing shooting and basketball IQ off the bench. Sophomore Lance Thomas brought toughness and defensive versatility.

The crown jewel of the freshmen was Singler, a 6-8 forward from Medford, Oregon, who had led South Medford High School to its first state championship by beating Kevin Love’s Lake Oswego team. He was the #4 recruit nationally and started at power forward from day one. Singler averaged 13.3 points and 5.9 rebounds and won ACC Rookie of the Year honors. He played with a quiet ferocity that didn’t announce itself with dunks or celebrations but showed up in every hustle play, every offensive rebound, and every willingness to guard bigger players. Nolan Smith, a freshman guard from Syracuse whose father Derek Smith had died of a heart attack on a cruise ship when Nolan was a child, provided depth in the backcourt and showed flashes of the dynamic scorer he would become.

The season started magnificently. Duke won the NIT Season Tip-Off in November, beating Princeton, Illinois, and Marquette in consecutive games. The Blue Devils rolled to a 21-1 start and climbed to #2 in the AP poll. For a stretch in January and February, this looked like the team that would bring Duke back to the Final Four. The defense was elite. The scoring was balanced across four double-figure contributors. Cameron Indoor was rocking again.

Then the floor shifted. Duke lost to UNC at Cameron Indoor Stadium as Hansbrough continued his undefeated reign in Durham. The Blue Devils went 7-5 over their final 12 regular-season and ACC Tournament games. The interior weakness that had plagued the program since Shelden Williams’ departure was exposed again and again — Duke had no answer for physical post play. The team could shoot, defend the perimeter, and compete in most games, but when opponents punished them inside, there was no counter.

Duke won its first game at UNC’s Dean Smith Center, beating the Tar Heels in a game where UNC was missing injured point guard Ty Lawson. It was Henderson’s lone win over Hansbrough in four career matchups. But UNC won the return game at Cameron, as Hansbrough and the Tar Heels continued their dominance in Durham.

In the ACC Tournament, Duke’s run ended in the semifinals, denying the team any momentum heading into March. The Blue Devils entered the NCAA Tournament as a #2 seed in the West Region — a strong seeding that reflected the 21-1 start more than the 7-5 finish.

The first-round game against 15th-seeded Belmont in Washington, D.C., nearly became one of the great upsets in tournament history. Duke survived 71-70 in a game that required every ounce of the team’s composure. It was the kind of game that championship-caliber teams win by 15 and rebuilding teams win by 1. Duke was clearly the latter.

The second round brought seventh-seeded West Virginia, and the physical mismatch was immediately apparent. The Mountaineers, led by Joe Alexander and Joe Mazzulla, pushed Duke around on both ends of the floor. They outrebounded the Blue Devils, controlled the paint, and played with a swaggering physicality that Duke couldn’t match. Alexander and Mazzulla seemed to enjoy the pushing and shoving, and Duke had no interior presence to push back. West Virginia won 73-67 in a game that wasn’t as close as the score suggested.

One Duke Basketball Report writer captured the sting perfectly: “It wasn’t losing as much as how Duke lost. Guys like Joe Alexander and Joe Mazzulla basically mocked the Blue Devils and pushed them around.”

The season ended at 28-6 — a significant improvement over the 22-11 nightmare of 2006-07, but still two consecutive years without a Sweet Sixteen appearance for the first time since the early 1990s. The post-Redick, post-Williams rebuilding project was taking longer than anyone had anticipated.

Nelson graduated and went undrafted but played professionally overseas. The core that would define Duke’s next chapter was now in place: Henderson (entering his junior year), Singler and Scheyer (sophomores), Thomas and Zoubek (juniors), and Smith (sophomore). They were still a year away from adding the final piece — but the identity was forming.

Meanwhile, K had taken on a second job. He had been named head coach of the United States men’s Olympic basketball team and would spend the summer of 2008 coaching LeBron James, Kobe Bryant, and Dwyane Wade to a gold medal in Beijing. The dual role was unprecedented for a college coach, and some critics wondered whether Team USA’s demands were splitting K’s focus. But the Olympic experience would reshape how K recruited, coached, and related to the next generation of NBA-bound talent. It was a bridge between the old Duke — built on four-year players and developmental arcs — and the new Duke that would eventually embrace one-and-done culture.

unc.story: Duke’s lone win over Hansbrough-era UNC at the Dean Smith Center. The Tar Heels were missing injured point guard Ty Lawson, who had suffered an ankle injury and would miss most of February. Without their floor general, UNC’s offense couldn’t find its rhythm, and Duke’s perimeter defense suffocated the remaining guards. It was Gerald Henderson’s only victory over Tyler Hansbrough in four career matchups — a detail Henderson noted years later on the podcast they would eventually share. Duke’s 21-1 start peaked here, with the Blue Devils ranked #2 in the country and looking like legitimate Final Four contenders.

Hansbrough continued his perfect record at Cameron Indoor Stadium. With Lawson healthy and back in the lineup, UNC was a different team than the one Duke had beaten in Chapel Hill a month earlier. The Tar Heels controlled the tempo and the glass, and Duke’s late-season slide continued. It was the Blue Devils’ sixth loss in their final twelve games. UNC, which would finish the regular season 32-2 and earn the #1 overall seed in the NCAA Tournament, looked every bit the best team in the country. They would reach the Final Four before a stunning loss to Kansas ended their season.

theGame.title: Pushed Around: West Virginia in the Second Round
theGame.headline: For the second consecutive year, Duke’s NCAA Tournament ends with a team that is more physical, more aggressive, and more comfortable in March. West Virginia’s Joe Alexander and Joe Mazzulla push the Blue Devils around, and Duke goes home without reaching the Sweet Sixteen for the second straight season.
theGame.story:
After barely surviving Belmont 71-70 in the first round — a game that required every ounce of a veteran team’s composure against a 15-seed — Duke drew seventh-seeded West Virginia in the second round. The Mountaineers were exactly the kind of team Duke couldn’t handle: big, physical, aggressive in the post, and utterly unafraid of the Duke name.

Joe Alexander, a 6-8 forward, and Joe Mazzulla, a scrappy guard who would later become an NBA head coach with the Boston Celtics, set the tone from the opening minutes. They punished Duke inside, outrebounded the Blue Devils, and played with a physical swagger that bordered on mockery. Alexander attacked the rim with impunity. Mazzulla competed with a snarling intensity that made every loose ball feel personal.

Duke had no answer. The interior weakness that had haunted the program since Shelden Williams’ graduation two years earlier was on full display. Singler, for all his skill, was giving away inches and pounds to West Virginia’s frontline. Zoubek was too foul-prone to stay on the floor. Henderson and Scheyer competed, but the game was being decided in the paint, not on the perimeter.

West Virginia won 73-67. The margin was six, but the game felt more lopsided than that. Duke had been physically overwhelmed for forty minutes.

It was the second consecutive year without a Sweet Sixteen appearance — the first time that had happened since 1995 and 1996, the years surrounding K’s back surgery and the 13-18 Pete Gaudet interim disaster. The 28-6 record and #2 seed had masked the reality: this team, for all its improvement over the VCU debacle, still lacked the toughness and interior presence to survive March against teams that could impose their will physically.

But the seeds were planted. Singler and Smith would return as sophomores. Henderson and Scheyer would enter their junior years. Thomas and Zoubek would be seniors in two years. The nucleus of the 2010 national championship team was assembling, even if nobody could see it yet through the frustration of another early exit.

K took the lesson to Beijing, where he spent the summer coaching NBA All-Stars. The experience would reshape his recruiting philosophy and his willingness to embrace elite one-and-done talent. The old Duke model — four-year players who grew into champions — was about to merge with a new reality. But first, there was one more year of growth to endure.

accTourney.story: Duke’s ACC Tournament run ended in the semifinals, denying the Blue Devils any championship momentum heading into March Madness. After a 21-1 start, the team had gone 7-5 down the stretch and couldn’t recapture the magic of early January.

ncaaTourney.story: A one-point survival against 15th-seeded Belmont that nearly became one of the great upsets in tournament history. Duke’s youth and late-season struggles were on full display in a game that never should have been this close. Championship teams win this game by 15. Rebuilding teams win it by 1. Duke was clearly the latter.

West Virginia physically dominated Duke for forty minutes. Joe Alexander and Joe Mazzulla pushed the Blue Devils around in the post, outrebounded them, and played with a swaggering physicality that Duke couldn’t match. The interior weakness that had plagued Duke since Shelden Williams’ graduation was exposed once more. Second consecutive year without a Sweet Sixteen. Second consecutive year ended by a more physical team. The message was clear: Duke needed toughness as badly as it needed talent.

seasons.tagline: The Last Step Before the Summit. Henderson becomes a star. Singler and Scheyer emerge as co-leaders. Duke wins the ACC Tournament and returns to the Sweet Sixteen for the first time in three years. Then Villanova reminds everyone that being close isn’t the same as being ready.
seasons.overview:
The 2008-09 season was the year the Transition finally started to look like it had a destination. Senior Greg Paulus moved to a bench role as sophomore Nolan Smith took over point guard duties, and the offense found a rhythm it had lacked for three years. Junior Gerald Henderson had his breakout season, averaging a team-high 15.1 points per game and establishing himself as a legitimate NBA prospect — a 6-5 wing with explosive athleticism, a developing mid-range game, and the defensive versatility that scouts coveted. Sophomore Kyle Singler co-led the scoring with Henderson at 16.5 points, cementing his place as Duke’s most complete player. Junior Jon Scheyer moved into a starting role and provided 14.9 points, sharpshooting, and a basketball IQ that made everyone around him better.

The roster also absorbed significant offseason losses. Associate head coach Johnny Dawkins left to become head coach at Stanford, severing a connection that dated to his playing days in the 1980s. Freshman Taylor King transferred to Villanova after one season — a departure that would become darkly ironic when Villanova ended Duke’s season in the Sweet Sixteen. Freshman McDonald’s All-American Elliot Williams and the Plumlee brothers (Miles as a freshman, with Mason committed for the following year) provided depth, but the frontcourt remained thin.

Duke started strong, winning 15 of its first 16 games. The lone early loss came to Michigan in the ACC-Big Ten Challenge. ACC play brought the familiar struggle: Duke went 11-5 in conference, losing both regular-season meetings to North Carolina — the same Tar Heel team that would go on to win the 2009 national championship behind Hansbrough, Lawson, Ellington, and Danny Green, one of the most dominant tournament runs in modern history (winning every game by double digits). The gap between Duke and UNC in 2008-09 was real: Carolina was playing for a championship while Duke was still searching for its identity.

But the ACC Tournament in Atlanta changed the narrative. Duke won three consecutive tight games — beating Boston College, Maryland, and Florida State to capture the championship. The title game victory over Florida State, 79-69, gave K his 11th ACC Tournament championship and tied Duke with UNC for the most tournament titles in conference history at 17. It was the program’s first ACC Tournament crown since 2006 and proof that this group could perform under pressure when the stakes were highest.

Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as a #2 seed in the East Region, its best seeding since 2006. The Blue Devils dismantled 15th-seeded Binghamton 86-62 in the first round and then survived a competitive 74-69 win over seventh-seeded Texas in the second round. For the first time since the Redick-Williams era, Duke was in the Sweet Sixteen.

Then came Villanova.

Jay Wright’s Wildcats were a third seed playing in Boston, and they brought the depth, athleticism, and defensive intensity that Duke simply couldn’t match. Villanova swarmed Henderson and Scheyer, forcing Duke into contested jumpers and difficult half-court possessions. Duke shot 9-of-35 from the field in the first half and never recovered. A Singler layup to open the second half cut it to 26-25, but Villanova responded with a 16-4 run that blew the game open at 42-29. Duke finished 3-of-16 from three-point range. The final score was 77-54 — a 23-point demolition that was Duke’s worst NCAA Tournament loss in years.

It wasn’t a buzzer-beater or a last-second heartbreak. It was a thorough dismantling by a team that was deeper, more physical, and more prepared for the moment. Villanova went on to reach the Final Four. Duke went home at 30-7, with the same nagging questions that had followed the program since 2006: were they tough enough? Deep enough? Physical enough to survive March against teams that could match their talent with superior bodies?

Henderson declared for the NBA Draft after the season and was selected 12th overall by the Charlotte Bobcats, K’s 37th NBA Draft pick. Paulus graduated, having never fully recaptured the promise of his pre-injury freshman year. Elliot Williams would transfer to Memphis after the season, another departure from a program in flux.

But the core remained. Scheyer would return as a senior. Singler as a junior. Smith as a junior. Thomas and Zoubek as seniors. The 2009-10 Duke Blue Devils would have five players who had been through the VCU first-round loss, the West Virginia beating, and the Villanova blowout — three consecutive years of tournament pain that had hardened them in ways that talent alone never could.

K looked at his returning roster and told people the team was “good, not great.” Nobody outside of Durham disagreed. Nobody inside of Durham cared. They had been through too much to need validation. They just needed one more chance.

The 2008-09 season was the last step before the summit. The climb had taken four years. The view from the top would be worth every scar.

unc.story: North Carolina dismantled Duke at Cameron Indoor Stadium in a game that laid bare the talent gap between the two programs. Ty Lawson’s relentless penetration was the story — he scored a game-high 25 points, with 21 coming in the second half when UNC broke the game open. The Tar Heels scored 101 points at Cameron, the first time Duke had allowed 100 on its home floor since 2000. It also assured that Hansbrough and Danny Green would finish their college careers undefeated at Cameron Indoor Stadium — a four-year perfect record in the building that was supposed to be Duke’s fortress. The Tar Heels were on their way to one of the most dominant seasons in college basketball history.

The regular season finale in Chapel Hill. With the ACC regular season championship on the line, UNC and Duke played a tightly contested first half before the Tar Heels pulled away. Hansbrough led UNC with 17 points, and Lawson fell just short of a triple-double as Carolina opened a late 10-point lead and cruised to a 79-71 victory. Scheyer had 24 and Singler 23 for Duke, but it wasn’t enough. UNC clinched the outright ACC regular season title and completed the season sweep. The Tar Heels would go on to win the 2009 national championship, routing every opponent in the tournament by double digits — one of the most dominant NCAA Tournament runs in history.

theGame.title: Villanova’s Demolition: The Sweet Sixteen Blowout
theGame.headline: Duke returns to the Sweet Sixteen for the first time in three years and gets annihilated. Villanova’s depth, physicality, and defensive pressure hold the Blue Devils to 9-of-35 shooting from the field and 3-of-16 from three. The final score is 77-54. It’s not close.
theGame.story:
For three years, the goal had been simple: get back to the Sweet Sixteen. The VCU first-round loss in 2007 and the West Virginia second-round exit in 2008 had been painful proof that the post-Redick, post-Williams rebuild was real and ongoing. When Duke beat Texas 74-69 in the second round to earn its first Sweet Sixteen appearance since 2006, the program exhaled. The drought was over.

Then Jay Wright’s Villanova Wildcats walked into the TD Banknorth Garden in Boston and administered the most thorough beating Duke had absorbed in an NCAA Tournament game in years.

The blueprint was now familiar to anyone who had watched Duke’s March exits: overwhelm the Blue Devils with depth, contest every three-pointer, force them into a halfcourt grind, and win the physical battles on the glass and in the lane. Villanova executed it to perfection. The Wildcats swarmed Henderson, Scheyer, and Singler — Duke’s Big Three — with rotating defenders, denying clean looks and forcing contested mid-range jumpers that clanked off iron.

Duke shot 9-of-35 from the field in the first half. Nine for thirty-five. The Blue Devils scored 24 first-half points and trailed 25-24 at the break — a low-scoring affair that might have favored Duke in other years. But this team didn’t have the interior toughness or defensive rebounding to survive an extended offensive drought.

Singler opened the second half with a layup to give Duke a 26-25 lead — the last time Duke would be within striking distance. Villanova responded with a devastating 16-4 run that stretched the lead to 42-29 and essentially ended the game with more than 15 minutes remaining. Sophomore guard Corey Stokes hit a three-pointer with two minutes left to punctuate the victory, and the Boston crowd began chanting “We want Pitt!”

The final score was 77-54. Duke shot 3-of-16 from beyond the arc. Henderson, the team’s best player and a future lottery pick, was contained. Scheyer couldn’t find his rhythm. Singler competed but had no help inside. Nobody in a Duke uniform could match Villanova’s depth — the Wildcats got contributions from seven players while Duke relied almost entirely on its top three.

It was a familiar ending to a different season. Three straight years of early NCAA Tournament exits, each one exposing the same structural weakness: Duke could shoot, defend the perimeter, and compete with talent, but they couldn’t survive against teams that were deeper, bigger, and more physical in the lane. The program that had built its dynasty on versatile frontcourt players like Laettner, Hill, Brand, Battier, Boozer, and Shelden Williams had spent three years without a genuine interior anchor.

Villanova went on to reach the Final Four, validating the blowout. Henderson declared for the NBA Draft and was selected 12th overall. Paulus graduated. Elliot Williams transferred.

But Scheyer, Singler, Smith, Thomas, and Zoubek remained. They had now been through VCU, West Virginia, and Villanova — three years of March pain that had burned the same lesson into their bones: talent without toughness dies in March. Being good isn’t enough. Being deep isn’t enough. You have to be hard.

K looked at his returning roster for 2009-10 and called them “good, not great.” He was being honest. He was also building the case for what would become the most together team he’d coached since 2001. Sometimes the best thing a coach can do is lower expectations and let hungry players prove everyone wrong.

The four-year climb was almost over. The summit was one season away.

accTourney.story: Duke opened the ACC Tournament in Atlanta with a hard-fought win over Boston College, beginning a three-game championship run that would announce the program’s return to elite status.

Duke survived a tight semifinal against Maryland, advancing to the championship game for the first time since 2006. The Blue Devils’ perimeter shooting and defensive intensity carried the day in a game that required every ounce of veteran poise.

Duke captured the ACC Tournament championship with a 79-69 victory over Florida State. It was K’s 11th ACC Tournament title and tied Duke with UNC for the most tournament championships in conference history at 17. Henderson, Singler, and Scheyer all contributed in a balanced offensive performance. The title was the program’s first since 2006 and validated a three-year rebuild. For the seniors and juniors who had lived through VCU and West Virginia, cutting down the nets in Atlanta felt like the first real evidence that the program was back.

ncaaTourney.story: A comfortable first-round win over Binghamton eased the lingering trauma of the Belmont scare from the previous year. Duke controlled the game from the opening tip, and the 24-point margin was the kind of decisive victory that tournament teams need to build confidence.

Duke survived a competitive game against a talented Texas team to reach the Sweet Sixteen for the first time since 2006 — ending a three-year drought that had felt like an eternity for a program accustomed to deep March runs. The five-point margin reflected the difficulty of the matchup, but Duke’s veteran core made the plays that mattered down the stretch.

The most thorough NCAA Tournament beating Duke had absorbed in years. Villanova’s depth and defensive pressure held the Blue Devils to 9-of-35 shooting and 3-of-16 from three. Duke led 26-25 after a Singler layup to open the second half. Then Villanova ripped off a 16-4 run and the game was over with 15 minutes remaining. Final: 77-54. Henderson, Scheyer, and Singler were all contained. Villanova went on to the Final Four. Duke went home with the same nagging questions: deep enough? Tough enough? Physical enough? Henderson declared for the NBA Draft (#12 pick). But Scheyer, Singler, Smith, Thomas, and Zoubek all returned. Three years of March pain had built something. The summit was one season away.

seasons.accTournament: Champions (record 18th ACC Tournament title; beat Georgia Tech in championship)
seasons.ncaaTournament: National Champions (#1 seed South; beat Arkansas-Pine Bluff 73-44, California 68-53, Purdue 70-57, Baylor 78-71, West Virginia 78-57, Butler 61-59)
seasons.tagline: Good, Not Great — Then Great. Four years of scars. VCU. West Virginia. Villanova. Five players who had been through all of it return and refuse to let the story end the same way. K calls them “good, not great.” They prove him wrong in the most beautiful way possible. Zoubek grabs the rebound. Hayward’s half-court shot hits the backboard, hits the rim, and bounces out. Duke wins its fourth national championship. The climb is over.
seasons.overview:
Mike Krzyzewski looked at his roster before the 2009-10 season and said what he believed: “They’re good, not great.” He wasn’t being modest. He was being honest. This team had no lottery picks, no future All-Stars, no singular talent that would make opponents change their game plan out of fear. What it had was something rarer and, in the end, more valuable: five players who had been through everything together.

Senior Jon Scheyer had arrived as a pudgy freshman in 2006, watched Redick and Williams play their Senior Night game, and spent four years transforming himself from a sixth man into a consensus second-team All-American and the ACC’s leader in assist-to-turnover ratio, free throw percentage, and three-pointers per game. He set the ACC all-time single-season record for minutes played at 1,470 — a number that reflected both his durability and his indispensability. He led the team in scoring (18.2), assists (4.9), free throw percentage (.878), and steals (1.6).

Junior Kyle Singler was the team’s most complete player, averaging 17.7 points and 7.0 rebounds with a .399 three-point percentage. He played with the quiet ferocity that had defined him since arriving from Medford, Oregon — never flashy, always essential, willing to guard any position and take any shot when the moment demanded it.

Junior Nolan Smith had grown from a raw freshman into a dynamic scorer and capable floor general, averaging 17.4 points and learning to play alongside Scheyer in a two-guard system that gave Duke unprecedented backcourt versatility.

Senior Lance Thomas brought defensive toughness and a willingness to do whatever the team needed, from guarding the opponent’s best player to setting screens to diving on the floor for loose balls. His hard foul on Butler’s Gordon Hayward in the championship game — reviewed and ruled non-flagrant — was the embodiment of the team’s identity: we will compete on every possession, and we will not apologize for it.

And then there was senior Brian Zoubek. The 7-1 center from New Jersey had spent four injury-plagued years on the margins of the program, never healthy enough to establish himself. In 2009-10, K inserted him into the starting lineup, and he blossomed. Zoubek averaged 7.6 rebounds and provided the interior presence that Duke had lacked since Shelden Williams’ departure — not as a scorer, but as a rebounder, shot-alterer, and physical enforcer. The Plumlee brothers (Miles as a sophomore, Mason as a freshman) added frontcourt depth that no Duke team since 2006 had possessed.

Between them, the five core players had played 638 games together. They knew each other’s movements, tendencies, and breaking points. They had survived VCU (first-round loss, 2007), West Virginia (physical beatdown, 2008), and Villanova (blowout, 2009). Three consecutive years of March failure had hardened them in ways that no practice could replicate.

The season started with a statement: Duke won the NIT Season Tip-Off and rolled through a non-conference schedule that included wins over Connecticut and Gonzaga. ACC play was dominant — 13-3, tied for the regular season championship. The Blue Devils went undefeated at Cameron Indoor Stadium for the entire season, setting a school record with 17 home wins. They won the ACC Tournament for a record 18th time, tying and then surpassing UNC for the most in conference history.

Duke entered the NCAA Tournament as the #1 seed in the South Region. The draw was kind but never easy.

The tournament run was methodical and relentless. Arkansas-Pine Bluff fell 73-44 in the first round. California went down 68-53 in the second round. Fourth-seeded Purdue was dispatched 70-57 in the Sweet Sixteen, with Zoubek grabbing 10 rebounds. Third-seeded Baylor fell 78-71 in the Elite Eight, with Lance Thomas pulling down 8 offensive rebounds in a physical, grinding affair.

The Final Four in Indianapolis brought a familiar opponent: second-seeded West Virginia. The same program that had physically manhandled Duke out of the tournament two years earlier. But this was a different Duke team. Zoubek was healthy and starting. The Plumlees provided interior reinforcement. Thomas and Singler were two years older and two years tougher. Duke demolished the Mountaineers 78-57 — the most emphatic victory of the tournament run and the sweetest revenge on the program’s four-year journey. Singler had 21 points. Scheyer added 23. The team committed just 6 turnovers against 20 assists. It was total domination.

The championship game against fifth-seeded Butler was unlike anything else. Butler, coached by 33-year-old Brad Stevens, was the real-life “Hoosiers” — a tiny school from Indianapolis playing for the national title in a stadium just miles from its campus. The crowd of 70,390 at Lucas Oil Stadium was overwhelmingly pro-Butler. Duke was the villain in someone else’s fairy tale.

Nobody led by more than six points. Butler shot 34.5% (20-of-58) but competed on every possession with the defensive intensity and team cohesion that had carried them through the tournament. Duke won with defense — holding Butler to its worst shooting night in months — and with clutch shot-making from Singler (19 points, 3-of-6 from three, 6-of-6 from the line in the second half). Zoubek finished with 10 rebounds and 2 blocks, altering shots at the rim all night.

The final sequence will live forever in college basketball history. With Duke leading 61-59 and 3.6 seconds remaining, Butler’s Gordon Hayward missed a shot and Zoubek grabbed the rebound. He was fouled immediately. Zoubek made the first free throw. He intentionally missed the second, knowing that a Butler three-pointer would only tie the game if Duke led by three rather than two. But the miss was long, and Butler got the ball at half-court.

Hayward took the inbound, dribbled twice, and launched a half-court heave as time expired. The ball flew toward the basket, hit the backboard, hit the rim — and bounced out.

70,390 people gasped. Then the Duke bench erupted.

“Both teams and all the kids on both teams played their hearts out,” Krzyzewski said. “There was never more than a couple, a few points separating, so a lot of kids made big plays for both teams.”

Singler was named Final Four Most Outstanding Player (averaging 20.0 points and 9.0 rebounds in the two games). Scheyer and Smith were named to the All-Final Four team. Scheyer, Singler, and Smith had each scored over 600 points during the season — only the second trio in Duke history to do so, after Jason Williams, Carlos Boozer, and Mike Dunleavy Jr. in 2001-02.

After the game, before the team said a prayer, K told them: “I’ve said throughout the year they were good, then they were really good, then they were really good with great character. But I told them before we said a prayer, that: ‘You are a great team.’”

It was K’s fourth national championship, tying Adolph Rupp for second on the all-time list behind John Wooden. It was Duke’s first title since 2001 — a nine-year drought that had included the Lakers drama, the Redick era’s unfulfilled promise, the VCU humiliation, and three years of rebuilding with a core that refused to break.

The 2009-10 national championship was not won by the most talented Duke team. It was won by the most together one — a team forged by failure, hardened by loss, and led by players who had been told they were good, not great, and decided to prove that sometimes good and together is enough to be great.

unc.story: The most lopsided Duke victory over UNC in the modern rivalry. Without Tyler Hansbrough and the senior class that had won the 2009 national championship, Roy Williams’ Tar Heels were in free fall — a young team that would finish 20-17, one of UNC’s worst seasons of the Williams era. Duke demolished them by 32 points at Cameron, the second-largest Duke win in the history of the series. It was a stark role reversal: a year earlier, UNC had scored 101 at Cameron. Now Duke was winning by 32. The balance of Tobacco Road power had shifted decisively back to Durham.

Duke completed the season sweep with another dominant victory at Chapel Hill — remarkably, another 82-50 scoreline, matching the margin from the game at Cameron exactly. The identical scores were an eerie coincidence that underlined how wide the gap had become between the two programs. Scheyer and Singler controlled the game. UNC, struggling through a rebuilding year, had no answer. It was the first time Duke had swept UNC in a season since 2002 and the first time both games had been blowouts since the Doherty-era collapses of 2001-02.

theGame.title: Hayward’s Shot Bounces Out: The Fourth Championship
theGame.headline: Duke 61, Butler 59. Zoubek grabs the rebound with 3.6 seconds left. Makes one free throw. Misses the second intentionally. Gordon Hayward launches from half court. It hits the backboard. It hits the rim. It bounces out. Duke wins its fourth national championship. The four-year climb is over.
theGame.story:
They had been through everything. VCU in 2007 — a first-round loss that ended a nine-year Sweet Sixteen streak. West Virginia in 2008 — a physical beating that exposed a team without toughness. Villanova in 2009 — a 23-point blowout that said: you’re still not ready. Three years of March pain, each one leaving a different scar.

Now they were in Indianapolis, in Lucas Oil Stadium, 70,390 people in the building and most of them rooting against Duke. Butler, coached by 33-year-old Brad Stevens, was the Cinderella story America wanted — a tiny school from the other side of Indianapolis, playing for a national championship in its own backyard, the real-life “Hoosiers” that Hollywood could never have scripted.

Duke was the villain. The Blue Devils embraced it.

The game was a masterpiece of tension. Nobody led by more than six. Butler shot 34.5% but competed with a ferocity that matched Duke’s own. Every possession was contested. Every rebound was fought for. Every timeout felt like a plot twist in a story that couldn’t possibly end cleanly.

Singler was Duke’s anchor: 19 points, 3-of-6 from three, 6-of-6 from the free throw line in the second half. He played all 40 minutes. Scheyer added 15 and ran the offense with the composure of a player who had waited four years for this moment. Zoubek was immense inside — 10 rebounds, 2 blocks, and countless altered shots that forced Butler into the low-percentage contested jumpers that defined their night.

Lance Thomas made the play that defined Duke’s identity. With 5:07 remaining, Hayward drove for what looked like an easy layup. Thomas took him down hard. The officials reviewed the play and ruled it non-flagrant. It was the physical, unyielding, we-will-not-let-you-score-easy toughness that this team had spent four years building. The team that had been pushed around by West Virginia in 2008 was pushing back.

With 3.6 seconds remaining, Duke led 60-59. Hayward missed a shot. Zoubek — the 7-1 senior who had spent four years fighting through injuries to arrive at this moment — grabbed the rebound. He was fouled immediately.

Zoubek made the first free throw. Duke led 61-59. He intentionally missed the second — a strategic decision to prevent Butler from getting the ball at half-court with a chance to tie with a three-pointer.

The miss was long. Butler corralled it at half-court. Hayward took the inbound and dribbled twice toward the Duke basket. With time expiring, he launched a half-court heave.

The ball sailed through the air. It hit the backboard. It hit the rim.

And it bounced out.

70,390 people gasped. The Duke bench erupted. Scheyer fell to the floor. Thomas threw his arms around Singler. K embraced his players. Zoubek, who had spent four years wondering if his body would ever let him play a full season, stood at center court with tears streaming down his face.

Butler’s Brad Stevens said it best: “We just came up a bounce short. There’s certainly nothing to hang your head about.”

K, in the locker room before the team said a prayer, delivered the line he had been building toward all season: “I’ve said throughout the year they were good, then they were really good, then they were really good with great character. But I told them that: ‘You are a great team.’”

Singler was named Final Four Most Outstanding Player (20.0 points, 9.0 rebounds per game in the two Final Four games). Scheyer and Smith were named to the All-Final Four team. It was K’s fourth national championship, tying Adolph Rupp for second all-time behind John Wooden.

The 2009-10 Duke Blue Devils were not the most talented championship team in program history. They had no future NBA All-Stars. They had no consensus National Player of the Year. They had no player selected in the first round of the NBA Draft that June. What they had was 638 games of shared experience, four years of accumulated scar tissue, and an identity that had been forged in the fires of three consecutive early tournament exits.

They were good, not great. Then they were great.

The four-year climb that began in the wreckage of VCU ended with confetti falling in Indianapolis. Every scar had been worth it.

accTourney.story: Duke won its record 18th ACC Tournament championship — surpassing UNC for the most in conference history. The title was the program’s third in four years (2009, 2010) and the 12th under K. Scheyer, Singler, and Smith all contributed in a balanced performance. The seniors cut down the nets knowing they had one more tournament to play — the one that mattered most.

ncaaTourney.story: A 29-point blowout to open the tournament. Duke was sharp, focused, and showed none of the anxiety that had plagued previous tournament openers. The 16-seed was dispatched without drama.

Duke controlled a competitive California team with defensive intensity and balanced scoring. A 15-point win that felt even more comfortable than the margin suggested. The Blue Devils advanced to the Sweet Sixteen — the round that had been their graveyard the previous year against Villanova.

The Sweet Sixteen demon was exorcised. Zoubek grabbed 10 rebounds as Duke’s improved interior presence neutralized Purdue’s physical frontcourt. The 13-point margin was the most comfortable Duke had looked in a Sweet Sixteen game since 2006. The team that had been eliminated in this round three straight years was now advancing past it with authority.

A physical, grinding Elite Eight battle against a talented Baylor team. Lance Thomas pulled down 8 offensive rebounds in a performance that embodied the team’s toughness. Duke survived Baylor’s size and athleticism with defensive discipline and free throw shooting. The Blue Devils were heading to the Final Four for the first time since 2004 — a six-year drought that had felt like a lifetime.

Revenge. The same West Virginia program that had physically dominated Duke out of the 2008 tournament. But this was a different Duke team: Zoubek healthy and anchoring the paint, the Plumlees providing frontcourt depth, Thomas and Singler two years tougher. Duke committed just 6 turnovers against 20 assists — a ratio of 3.33, the best of the season — and demolished the Mountaineers 78-57. Singler had 21. Scheyer added 23. It was total, methodical domination. The team that had been pushed around in 2008 was doing the pushing. The ghost of Buffalo was finally buried.

The game that had everything. Butler, the real-life “Hoosiers,” playing in its own backyard in Indianapolis. 70,390 fans, most rooting against Duke. Nobody led by more than six. Singler’s 19 points (6-of-6 FTs in the second half). Thomas’s hard foul on Hayward at 5:07. Zoubek’s 10 rebounds and 2 blocks. Then the final sequence: Hayward misses, Zoubek grabs the rebound with 3.6 seconds left, makes one free throw, intentionally misses the second. Hayward launches from half court. It hits the backboard. Hits the rim. Bounces out. Duke 61, Butler 59. Fourth national championship. K ties Adolph Rupp. And in the locker room, before a prayer: “You are a great team.”

seasons.tagline: The Greatest What-If. The defending national champions return two All-Americans, add the #1 recruit in the country — and lose him after eight games. Kyrie Irving jams his toe against Butler on December 4 and disappears for 26 games. Nolan Smith becomes the ACC Player of the Year. Seth Curry grows up overnight. Duke goes 24-2 without its superstar freshman and wins a third straight ACC Tournament championship. Irving returns for the NCAAs, scores 28 in the Sweet Sixteen, but it isn’t enough. Derrick Williams drops 32 and Arizona ends the dream 93-77. Seven future NBA players were on this roster. The team that might have gone undefeated instead became college basketball’s most tantalizing what-if.
seasons.overview:
The 2010-11 Duke Blue Devils entered the season as the defending national champions, ranked #1 in the preseason AP poll, and armed with a roster that looked, on paper, like the most talented team Mike Krzyzewski had ever assembled. Seniors Nolan Smith and Kyle Singler returned as proven stars from the championship run. The Plumlee brothers — junior Miles and sophomore Mason — anchored the frontcourt. Sophomore Seth Curry and sophomore Andre Dawkins provided backcourt depth. Sophomore Ryan Kelly added stretch-four versatility. And arriving as the crown jewel of the recruiting class was Kyrie Irving, the #1 overall recruit in America, a point guard from St. Patrick’s in New Jersey whose handle, vision, and scoring ability were already drawing Chris Paul comparisons.

For eight games, the dream was real. Irving averaged 17.4 points on 53.2% shooting with 5.1 assists, 3.8 rebounds, and 1.5 steals per game. Duke was 8-0, obliterating everyone in its path. The Smith-Irving-Singler trio looked unstoppable — three future NBA players sharing backcourt responsibilities, each capable of creating for himself and others, each deadly from outside. Cleveland Cavaliers scouts attended every game, already locked in on Irving as a potential #1 pick.

Then, on December 4, 2010, in a rematch of the national championship game against Butler, Irving jammed his right big toe. He went back in and finished the game. It seemed like nothing — a stubbed toe, the kind of injury that happens a dozen times a season. “I feel like he just stubbed his toe,” the team’s athletic trainer recalled. “Something you see in a lot of basketball games.” But the injury didn’t heal. The ligament damage was worse than anyone initially realized. Irving missed 26 games. The #1 recruit in the country, the player who was supposed to make Duke unbeatable, was gone.

What happened next proved the character of this team. Rather than collapse, Duke adapted. Seth Curry, a quiet sophomore from the basketball family that produced his older brother Steph, moved into the starting point guard role and played with poise far beyond his experience. Nolan Smith, freed from sharing playmaking duties, unleashed the full scope of his game. He became the first player in ACC history to lead the conference in both scoring and assists in the same season, averaging 20.6 points, 5.1 assists, 4.5 rebounds, and 1.2 steals per game. He scored a career-high 34 points in a home win over #20 North Carolina, making 13 field goals. He scored 25 or more points in ten games and 18 or more in all sixteen ACC games. He was named ACC Player of the Year, a consensus first-team All-American, and a finalist for the Naismith Trophy, Wooden Award, Oscar Robertson Trophy, and Bob Cousy Award. He was the son of the late Derek Smith, a member of Louisville’s 1980 championship team who died in 1996 when Nolan was eight years old. The tattoo on his right arm reads: “Forever Watching.” His senior season was a masterpiece born from necessity.

Kyle Singler, the steady, unflashy Oregonian who never missed a big moment, averaged 16.9 points and 6.3 rebounds while earning first-team All-ACC honors for the second consecutive season. Together, Smith and Singler carried the load through conference play, going 13-3 in the ACC and finishing as co-regular-season champions with North Carolina.

The UNC rivalry produced a classic split. At Cameron Indoor on February 9, Smith erupted for 34 points in a 79-73 victory — a performance that announced his candidacy for every national award. At Chapel Hill on March 5, UNC exacted revenge on Senior Night with an 81-67 win that clinched the outright regular-season title. Smith scored 30 and Curry added 20, but the Tar Heels, led by Harrison Barnes’ 18 and Kendall Marshall’s 15 points and 11 assists, pulled away in the second half. Duke got the last word the following week, beating Maryland, Virginia Tech, and UNC in the ACC Tournament to claim the program’s record 19th conference tournament championship and its third in a row.

As the #1 seed in the West Region, Duke entered the NCAA Tournament with a tantalizing storyline: Kyrie Irving was coming back. He had missed 26 games, hadn’t played competitive basketball since early December, and was attempting to integrate back into a team that had learned to win without him. In the first round against Hampton, Irving came off the bench and led the team with 14 points in 20 minutes. Duke won 87-45. In the second round against Michigan, Smith scored 24 as Duke survived 73-71 — a game that also gave K his 900th career victory, making him only the fourth coach to reach that milestone.

The Sweet Sixteen, held in Anaheim, brought #5-seeded Arizona and its transcendent sophomore, Derrick Williams. Duke led 44-38 at halftime despite Williams scoring 25 first-half points, including going 5-for-5 from three-point range. But in the second half, Arizona erupted for 55 points. Williams finished with 32 and 13 rebounds in a career-defining performance. Irving, playing in only his eleventh game of the season, was magnificent in defeat — 28 points on 8-of-17 shooting — but the chemistry that might have existed with a full season of development simply wasn’t there. Arizona won 93-77. “I’ve never seen Duke get beat like this,” Kenny Smith said on the broadcast. “I’ve never seen a Duke team this rattled.”

The season ended at 32-5. Kyrie Irving declared for the NBA Draft and was selected #1 overall by the Cleveland Cavaliers. Nolan Smith went 21st to the Portland Trail Blazers. Kyle Singler went 33rd to the Detroit Pistons. In total, seven players from this roster would play in the NBA: Irving, Smith, Singler, Mason Plumlee, Miles Plumlee, Seth Curry, and Ryan Kelly.

For Duke fans, the 2010-11 season lives in a permanent state of torment and wonder. The belief — held by Krzyzewski, by analysts, by anyone who watched those first eight games — is that a healthy Kyrie Irving would have produced an undefeated season and back-to-back national championships. Duke Basketball Report put it bluntly: “It’s our firm belief that the Blue Devils were poised for an undefeated season when Irving went out with a toe injury and a second set of back-to-back national championships.” Instead, a stubbed toe — just a stubbed toe — changed everything. The greatest what-if in Duke basketball history.
